perturbation.original_text: All prior negotiations, representations and agreements with respect thereto not incorporated in this Amendment or the Distributor Agreement are hereby canceled.
perturbation.changed_text: All prior negotiations, representations and agreements with respect thereto not incorporated in this Amendment are hereby canceled.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'or the Distributor Agreement' now opens to ambiguity. While the intent might still be for the Distributor Agreement to remain consistent, removing this section makes it less clear whether or not this clause applies to the original agreement and all previous negotiations of that agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 29 CFR § 1625.22 - Waivers of rights and claims under the ADEA.
perturbation.law_explanation: This relates to agreements like this that might waive rights under the ADEA. If the original agreement is ambiguously cancelled by this clause, then it is possible this clause could void a waiver of rights. This section states such waivers have to be clear and understandable, and this amendment makes it unclear if the clause is intended to affect the original agreement.

Home statutes Age Discrimination in Employment Act of 1967 Age Discrimination in Employment Act of 1967 EDITOR'S NOTE: The following is the text of the Age Discrimination in Employment Act of 1967 (Pub. L. 90-202) (ADEA). The ADEA is codified at 29 U.S.C. 621 and in subsequent sections. Amendments to the ADEA include those introduced by the Older Workers Benefit Protection Act of 1990 and the Civil Rights Act of 1991 . Editor's notes appear in italics and include cross references to the codified version of the ADEA. The ADEA prohibits employment discrimination against persons 40 years of age or older. An Act To prohibit age discrimination in employment. Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled, that this Act may be cited as the "Age Discrimination in Employment Act of 1967." * * * CONGRESSIONAL STATEMENT OF FINDINGS AND PURPOSE SEC. 621. [Section 2] (a) The Congress hereby finds and declares that- (1) in the face of rising productivity and affluence, older workers find themselves disadvantaged in their efforts to retain employment, and especially to regain employment when displaced from jobs; (2) the setting of arbitrary age limits regardless of potential for job performance has become a common practice, and certain otherwise desirable practices may work to the disadvantage of older persons; (3) the incidence of unemployment, especially long-term unemployment with resultant deterioration of skill, morale, and employer acceptability is, relative to the younger ages, high among older workers; their numbers are great and growing; and their employment problems grave; (4) the existence in industries affecting commerce, of arbitrary discrimination in employment because of age, burdens commerce and the free flow of goods in commerce. (b) It is therefore the purpose of this chapter to promote employment of older persons based on their ability rather than age; to prohibit arbitrary age discrimination in employment; to help employers and workers find ways of meeting problems arising from the impact of age on employment. EDUCATION AND RESEARCH PROGRAM; RECOMMENDATION TO CONGRESS SEC. 622. [Section 3] (a) The EEOC [originally, the Secretary of Labor] s
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a structural flaw leading to a potential legal contradiction, by removing "or the Distributor Agreement" from the original clause. The Law Explanation suggests this change could ambiguously cancel the original agreement, potentially affecting waivers of rights under the ADEA. The ADEA, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 2, aims to prohibit age discrimination in employment (""). If the amendment makes it unclear whether the original agreement, which might contain a waiver, is still valid, then the modified clause could contradict the intent of the ADEA. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to waivers of rights under the ADEA, indicating the importance of clarity in such waivers. The modified text makes the scope of the cancellation unclear, potentially undermining this clarity and creating a legal contradiction.
